label: Dense Retrieval
definition: Retrieval that represents queries and documents as dense embedding vectors and ranks by similarity in that vector space, matching on meaning rather than on shared words.

body.text: Dense retrieval ranks documents by the closeness of dense vectors, the embeddings produced by an embedding model, rather than by overlapping words. "Dense" describes the representation: every dimension carries a real-valued signal, so a passage is a compact point of a few hundred to a few thousand numbers in which meaning is distributed across the whole vector. A query is embedded into the same space, and the documents whose vectors lie nearest, usually by cosine similarity, are returned. This is the retrieval method underneath semantic search and the default first stage in modern retrieval-augmented generation.

The representation is what separates it from the alternative. Dense retrieval stands opposite sparse retrieval, where a document is a very wide, mostly-zero vector over the entire vocabulary, with weights only on the words it contains. Sparse methods match exact terms; dense methods match concepts, so a dense retriever can connect "car" to "automobile" and a question to its answer even with no shared words. The dense encoder is typically a bi-encoder, embedding query and document independently so all document vectors can be precomputed and indexed, which is what makes dense retrieval fast at query time even over millions of passages.

Dense retrieval matters because semantic generalization, the ability to retrieve on meaning across vocabulary mismatch, is exactly what bare keyword search lacks, and it is the property that makes RAG robust to how a user phrases a question. The encoder learns that meaning through contrastive learning, trained on pairs that should be near and pairs that should be far. Its weaknesses are the inverse of its strengths: it can underperform on rare proper nouns, exact identifiers, and precise codes, where literal term matching is what you want, and its quality leans on whether the training data resembled the target domain. Those gaps are why production systems often pair it with sparse scoring in hybrid search and add a reranking stage.

This site is built on dense retrieval. Every corpus chunk is embedded with voyage-3 into a 1024-dimensional vector and stored in Pinecone, and both Explore search and the retrieval feeding the daily briefing rank by cosine similarity in that dense space. The same dense vectors are reused well beyond search, to bind concepts in the AI-grown taxonomy and to drive the clustering and rotation layout, which shows how a single dense representation can serve retrieval and organization at once.
